titleStmt.title: The Wretched of the Earth — TEI Encoding

publicationStmt.p: Encoded for the course Information Science and Cultural Heritage, University of Bologna, a.y. 2025–2026.

projectDesc.p: This TEI document encodes an abridged text based on the Wikipedia article on The Wretched of the Earth (1961) by Frantz Fanon. Entities including persons, places, organisations, events and concepts are annotated and reconciled to authority files including VIAF, Wikidata, GeoNames and Getty AAT.

samplingDecl.p: The encoded body text is an abridged adaptation derived from the English Wikipedia article "The Wretched of the Earth" (CC BY-SA 4.0). Only representative passages were retained and lightly condensed; no claim is made that the text reproduces the source article verbatim. The complete set of identified entities is recorded in the stand-off lists of the back matter and is independent of this sampling.

editorialDecl.p: Text is based on, and abridged from, the English Wikipedia article. At least 10 entities have been annotated using TEI elements. All real-world entities are linked to authority files via the @ref attribute.

div.p: The Wretched of the Earth (Les Damnés de la Terre) is a 1961 non-fiction book by Frantz Fanon, a psychiatrist and political philosopher from Martinique. The book addresses the psychological and political effects of colonialism on colonised peoples and theorises the necessity of decolonisation as a complete transformation of the social order.

The book was first published in French by François Maspero in 1961, with a preface by the French philosopher Jean-Paul Sartre. The first English translation was published by Grove Press in 1963, translated by Constance Farrington. A revised translation by Richard Philcox, with a foreword by Homi K. Bhabha, was published in 2004.

div.head: Overview
div.p: Fanon wrote The Wretched of the Earth during the Algerian War of Independence, while working as a psychiatrist in Algeria and serving as a member of the Front de Libération Nationale (FLN). The book draws on his clinical experience of treating both colonial soldiers and their victims.

The first chapter, "On Violence", is the book's most controversial section. It opens with the declaration:
Decolonization is always a violent phenomenon.
Fanon argues that colonial rule is itself founded on violence, and that anticolonial violence can serve as a psychological and political act of liberation for the colonised. The chapter drew both endorsement from Sartre in his preface and fierce criticism from liberal thinkers across Europe and North America.

Fanon also critiques the Négritude movement, founded by his former teacher Aimé Césaire from Martinique. While acknowledging its cultural importance, Fanon argues that Négritude risks essentialising Blackness and is ultimately insufficient as a political programme for decolonisation.

div.head: Historical context
div.p: The book was written in the context of the Algerian Revolution (1954–1962), one of the defining anticolonial struggles of the twentieth century. Algeria had been a French colony since 1830, and the war for independence resulted in approximately one and a half million Algerian deaths.

Several of the book's key arguments originated in a speech Fanon delivered at the Second Congress of Black Writers and Artists held in Rome, 1959, organised by the journal Présence Africaine. The speech, later reworked into the chapter "On National Culture", examined the role of intellectuals in the process of decolonisation. It is the source of one of the book's most quoted lines:
Each generation must, out of relative obscurity, discover its mission, fulfil it, or betray it.

div.head: Reception and legacy
div.p: The Wretched of the Earth was widely read by anticolonial movements across Africa, Asia, and Latin America in the 1960s and 1970s. The preface by Jean-Paul Sartre was later removed from some editions after Sartre's public support for Israel during the Six-Day War of 1967 led Josie Fanon, Fanon's widow, to request its removal.

The 2004 edition published by Grove Press includes a new foreword by postcolonial theorist Homi K. Bhabha, who reinterprets Fanon's legacy through the lens of poststructuralism, introducing concepts of hybridity and ambivalence into the reading of the text.

person.note: Author of The Wretched of the Earth. Member of the FLN.
Wrote the preface to the 1961 edition of The Wretched of the Earth.
Wrote the foreword to the 2004 edition of The Wretched of the Earth.
Co-founder of the Négritude movement. Fanon's teacher.

place.note: Caribbean island and French overseas territory. Birthplace of Frantz Fanon and Aimé Césaire.
Country in North Africa, formerly a French colony from 1830 to 1962. Central context of The Wretched of the Earth.

org.note: American independent publishing house. Published the first English translation of The Wretched of the Earth in 1963.

event.label: Algerian Revolution
event.desc: The war of independence fought between Algeria and France, 1954–1962. The direct historical context in which Fanon wrote The Wretched of the Earth.

event.label: Second Congress of Black Writers and Artists
event.desc: International gathering of Black intellectuals held in Rome in 1959. Fanon's speech there became the basis for key sections of The Wretched of the Earth.

item.gloss: A system of political and economic domination in which one country exerts control over another. Central object of critique in The Wretched of the Earth.
The process by which colonised peoples reclaim political independence and cultural identity. For Fanon, a complete transformation of the social order.
A literary and intellectual movement celebrating Black African culture and identity, founded by Aimé Césaire and Léopold Sédar Senghor. Critiqued by Fanon in The Wretched of the Earth.

bibl.title: The Wretched of the Earth
bibl.author: Frantz Fanon
bibl.note: First English translation by Constance Farrington, Grove Press, 1963.

bibl.title: "On Violence"
bibl.note: Opening chapter of The Wretched of the Earth. Argues that colonial rule is founded on violence and that anticolonial violence can serve as liberation.
